era_context: Continuation of the Old Kingdom's final phase in Egypt and Akkadian collapse in Mesopotamia. The Indus Valley Civilization remains at its mature peak. In China, the Xia Dynasty (if historical) is in its early centuries, though no contemporary written records survive. The broader context is one of transition: established empires fragmenting, regional powers consolidating, and long-distance trade networks persisting despite political upheaval.

events.title: Continued Akkadian fragmentation; Ur-Nammu's rise in southern Mesopotamia
events.description: As Akkadian central authority collapses, regional strongmen emerge. Ur-Nammu, founder of the Ur III dynasty, begins consolidating power in the southern city of Ur. Though traditionally dated to c. 2112 BCE, some scholars argue for an earlier emergence of Ur-Nammu's authority in this period. The transition from Akkadian to Ur III hegemony is gradual, with Gutian rulers still controlling much of central and northern Mesopotamia. Ur-Nammu's rise represents the beginning of a new phase of Mesopotamian political organization centered on Sumerian city-states.
key_figures: Ur-Nammu (founder of Ur III dynasty, traditional r. c. 2112–2095 BCE)

sources.name: Ur III administrative texts and inscriptions

events.certainty_note: The traditional dating of Ur-Nammu's reign is c. 2112–2095 BCE, placing his rise in the -2120s to -2110s. Assigning his emergence to -2158 is speculative and depends on whether earlier Ur III texts can be reliably dated to this period. The chronology is uncertain by ±50 years.
cross_references: Leads to Ur III hegemony (c. -2112 to -2004)

events.title: Harappan maritime trade networks at maximum extent
events.region: Indus Valley and Arabian Sea trade routes
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization's maritime trade reaches its peak. Harappan seals and artifacts appear in Mesopotamian contexts (Akkad, Lagash, Ur), the Persian Gulf, and Oman. Indus merchants establish trading posts or colonies at Dilmun (Bahrain) and possibly Magan (Oman). This trade is facilitated by standardized weights and measures, a sophisticated maritime technology, and possibly a shared understanding of commercial protocols. The trade in lapis lazuli, carnelian, and other luxury goods enriches both Harappan and Mesopotamian elites.

sources.name: Harappan seals found in Mesopotamian contexts (Nippur, Lagash, Ur)
Mesopotamian cuneiform texts mentioning Meluhha, Dilmun, and Magan
Archaeological evidence from Dholavira and coastal Harappan sites

events.certainty_note: The existence of Harappan-Mesopotamian trade is well-attested by archaeological and textual evidence. Dating to this specific year is approximate, based on correlation with Akkadian chronology.
cross_references: Precedes Harappan decline and trade network collapse c. -1900

events.title: Egyptian First Intermediate Period: regional autonomy increases
events.description: The First Intermediate Period (c. 2180–2055 BCE) is well underway. The Seventh and Eighth Dynasties, based in Memphis, exercise nominal authority over a fragmented Egypt. Regional governors in Upper Egypt (Thebes, Aswan) and Lower Egypt (Delta) operate as independent rulers, minting their own inscriptions and conducting their own military campaigns. Nomarchs such as Ankhtifi of Hierakonpolis and Sirenput of Aswan record their deeds in autobiographical inscriptions that emphasize local power and regional conflicts rather than loyalty to a distant pharaoh.

sources.name: Abydos King List and Turin Papyrus
Archaeological evidence of reduced central authority and local monument construction

events.certainty_note: The broad narrative of First Intermediate Period decentralization is well-attested. Precise dating of individual nomarchs' reigns is uncertain by ±20–50 years.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of the First Intermediate Period as a time of chaos and decline is increasingly nuanced by modern scholarship. Recent studies suggest that regional autonomy and local prosperity may have coexisted with reduced central authority, and that the period was not uniformly impoverished or unstable. The 'famine' narrative is similarly contested. Additionally, the dating of Ur-Nammu's rise is debated; some scholars place it significantly later than -2158, in the -2120s or -2110s, which would shift the chronology of Akkadian-to-Ur III transition.
historiographic_note: The historical record for this year remains heavily dependent on king lists, autobiographical inscriptions, and archaeological stratigraphy, all of which carry significant dating uncertainty. Egyptian sources are more abundant than Mesopotamian sources for this period, creating a potential bias toward Egyptian history. The Indus Valley Civilization's undeciphered script limits understanding of political and social developments. Chinese history for this period is entirely dependent on traditional chronologies (Xia Dynasty) that lack contemporary written confirmation. The broader picture is one of transition and fragmentation, but the precise chronology remains contested.

graph_edges.note: Ur-Nammu's consolidation of power leads to Ur III dynasty hegemony
Peak maritime trade precedes collapse of Harappan civilization
First Intermediate Period fragmentation precedes Middle Kingdom reunification
